original_hypothesis.title: withdraw() Checks-Effects-Interactions Violation Enables Reentrancy Drain
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The withdraw() function calls IERC20(token).safeTransfer(msg.sender, amount) BEFORE updating balances[msg.sender][token] and totalDeposits[token]. If the token supports transfer callbacks (ERC777, ERC677, or tokens with hooks), an attacker can re-enter withdraw() during the transfer. Since the balance hasn't been decremented, the sufficiency check passes again, allowing repeated withdrawals draining all deposited tokens of that type.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The CEI violation is clearly visible in the code: external call at the start, state updates after. The attack_realism is MEDIUM because exploitation requires the deposited token to have callback functionality—standard ERC20 tokens without hooks are not vulnerable. However, TIP-20 tokens with memo fields and policy integration (per documentation) may support callbacks.
deployment_assumptions: Deposited token must support transfer callbacks (ERC777, ERC677, or custom hooks)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Documentation mentions TIP-20 tokens with 'memo fields and policy integration' and 'TIP-403 Transfer Policy' which may include callback mechanisms. No deployment script available to verify specific token implementations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Line order: (1) safeTransfer to msg.sender, (2) balances -= amount, (3) totalDeposits -= amount. External call precedes state update—classic CEI violation

preconditions: Attacker has deposited at least some amount of the callback-enabled token
Contract holds sufficient token balance from other depositors to drain

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: withdraw(): safeTransfer(msg.sender, amount) at line ~160; balances[msg.sender][token] -= amount at line ~161
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Deposit X tokens → withdraw(token, X) → during callback re-enter withdraw(token, X) → repeat until contract drained
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Each re-entry withdraws full balance again; profit = (contract_balance - initial_deposit)

economic_questions: Do TIP-20 tokens support transfer hooks or callbacks?
What is the reentrancy depth limit given gas constraints?
Are there any tokens on the Tempo chain with ERC777 compatibility?
validation_requirements: Verify TIP-20 token transfer mechanism for callback support
Test reentrancy via malicious token contract with safeTransfer callback
Calculate maximum drain per reentrancy chain given gas limits

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal deposit of callback-enabled token (e.g., 1 token)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.01 ETH gas (multiple re-entry calls in one tx)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 100% of all deposited callback-enabled tokens
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Near-infinite if callback tokens are widely deposited

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete drain of all callback-enabled token deposits

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All depositors of the callback-enabled token

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify TIP-20 callback mechanism from Tempo protocol docs
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test reentrancy depth vs gas limit on Tempo chain

validation_result.impact_summary: The withdraw() function performs an external token transfer before updating internal balance state. If a callback-enabled token is used, an attacker can re-enter withdraw() repeatedly, draining all deposited tokens of that type from the contract.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The withdraw() function at StablecoinDEX.sol lines 153-163 performs operations in the wrong order: (1) Checks balance sufficiency, (2) Calls IERC20(token).safeTransfer(msg.sender, amount) — an external call, (3) Only then updates balances[msg.sender][token] -= amount and totalDeposits[token] -= amount. This is a textbook Checks-Effects-Interactions (CEI) violation. During the safeTransfer call, if the token invokes a callback to msg.sender (e.g., ERC777 tokensReceived hook, ERC677 onTokenTransfer, or any token with transfer hooks), the attacker's contract can re-enter withdraw(). Because balances[msg.sender][token] has not yet been decremented, the balance sufficiency check on line 156 passes again with the same stale value, allowing the attacker to withdraw the same amount repeatedly. Each re-entrant call transfers tokens before any state is updated, and the state updates only occur as the call stack unwinds. By that point, multiple transfers have already been executed. Notably, the contract's own _processWithdrawal function (used by emergencyWithdraw) correctly performs state updates BEFORE the external call, further demonstrating that the developer was aware of the correct pattern but failed to apply it consistently in withdraw().
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The CEI violation is unambiguous in the source code. Line 158 performs the external call (safeTransfer), while lines 159-160 perform the state updates (balance and totalDeposits decrements). There is no reentrancy guard (no ReentrancyGuard modifier, no mutex lock, no nonReentrant). The balance check on line 156 reads stale state during any re-entrant call. This is a clear code defect in the ordering of operations.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: safeTransfer at line ~158 executes before balances decrement at line ~159. During the transfer, if the token triggers a callback to msg.sender, the attacker re-enters withdraw(). The balance check at line ~156 still sees the original (un-decremented) balance, allowing the withdrawal to succeed again. This repeats until the contract's token balance is exhausted.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker deposits callback-enabled tokens via deposit() — balances[attacker][token] = X
4. Line 158: IERC20(token).safeTransfer(attacker, X) — external call triggers callback
6. Line 156: Check passes AGAIN — balances[attacker][token] is STILL X (not yet decremented)
7. Line 158: Another safeTransfer(attacker, X) — another callback, another re-entry
8. Steps 5-7 repeat until contract balance is exhausted or gas runs out
9. Call stack unwinds: each frame executes line 159 (balances -= X), causing arithmetic underflow revert in Solidity 0.8+

NOTE: The underflow on unwinding would cause revert in standard Solidity 0.8, BUT the attacker can calibrate: deposit enough to cover N withdrawals (deposit N*X, then withdraw X repeatedly N+k times where k drains others' funds), OR use the existing emergencyWithdraw unchecked underflow to pre-inflate their balance
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The core defect is the ordering at lines 158-160. The external call at line 158 precedes state updates at lines 159-160. For exploitation with standard Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic, the attacker must ensure the unwinding decrements don't underflow (e.g., by having enough balance to cover all re-entrant decrements, or by combining with the emergencyWithdraw underflow bug). However, the CEI violation itself is a definitive code defect regardless of exploit feasibility. The correct fix is to move lines 159-160 before line 158, matching the pattern already used in _processWithdrawal.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Deposited token must support transfer callbacks (ERC777, ERC677, or custom hooks)
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that specify which tokens will be listed. foundry.toml only configures compilation settings. Documentation references TIP-20 tokens with 'memo fields and policy integration' and 'TIP-403 Transfer Policy' — callback support is unknown but plausible.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack requires a token with transfer callbacks. If such a token is deposited: (1) Attacker deposits amount X of callback-token. (2) Attacker calls withdraw(token, X). (3) During safeTransfer, callback triggers re-entry into withdraw(token, X). (4) Balance check passes because state is stale. (5) Process repeats. The Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic would cause a revert on unwinding if total withdrawn exceeds the attacker's original balance. However, the attacker can combine this with the emergencyWithdraw underflow vulnerability (which uses unchecked arithmetic) to pre-inflate their balance to near uint128.max, making the unwinding decrements safe. Alternatively, if only standard tokens are used, the CEI violation is still a code defect even without a complete exploit chain.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If a callback-enabled token is used with this DEX, an attacker can drain all deposits of that token type. Combined with the emergencyWithdraw unchecked underflow bug (which can pre-inflate balances), the reentrancy becomes fully exploitable even with Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic on unwinding. Impact: complete loss of all deposited callback-capable tokens.
recommended_mitigations: Move state updates before the external call: balances[msg.sender][token] -= amount; totalDeposits[token] -= amount; IERC20(token).safeTransfer(msg.sender, amount); — matching the pattern already used in _processWithdrawal
Add OpenZeppelin ReentrancyGuard with nonReentrant modifier to withdraw(), emergencyWithdraw(), and deposit()
Consider using a pull-payment pattern or adding a reentrancy lock as defense-in-depth
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Create a mock ERC20 token with a transfer hook that calls back into the DEX. 2. Deploy StablecoinDEX. 3. Have victim deposit 100 tokens. 4. Have attacker deposit 10 tokens. 5. Attacker calls withdraw(token, 10) — hook re-enters withdraw(token, 10) up to 10 more times. 6. Verify attacker received 110 tokens (draining victim's deposit). Note: May need to combine with emergencyWithdraw pre-inflation to avoid checked arithmetic revert on unwinding.
uncertainties: Whether TIP-20 tokens on Tempo chain support transfer callbacks is unverifiable from provided materials
The exact depth of re-entrancy achievable depends on gas limits on the Tempo chain
Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic may cause revert on stack unwinding unless combined with emergencyWithdraw underflow exploit
additional_testing_needed: Deploy with ERC777-compatible token and attempt reentrancy exploit
Test combined attack: emergencyWithdraw underflow + withdraw reentrancy
Measure maximum reentrancy depth given Tempo chain gas limits
Verify whether TIP-20 token standard includes transfer hooks
